metadata.title: Dario Amodei — "We are near the end of the exponential"

summary_zh: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei 深度剖析 scaling law 现状、AGI 1-3年时间线、算力采购的生死赌局、AI 行业经济学，以及安全治理与出口管制的地缘博弈。

topics.title: Big Blob of Compute 假说：RL scaling 不是新范式，而是预训练的延伸
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 认为 RL scaling 与预训练 scaling 本质相同，都遵循他 2017 年在 OpenAI 写下的"Big Blob of Compute"假说——只有七个要素真正重要，一切"聪明的技巧"都不重要。

- 七要素包括：原始算力总量、数据数量、数据质量与分布广度、训练时长、可无限扩展的目标函数（预训练目标函数和 RL 目标函数各算一类）、以及归一化/条件化等数值稳定性技巧。Dario 表示自 2017 年至今没有看到任何与该假说矛盾的证据。
- RL scaling 正在重复预训练的进化路径：从单一任务（如数学竞赛 AIME）到更广泛的任务（代码、多领域 RL），最终将像预训练从 GPT-1 到 GPT-2 那样实现泛化。多家公司已公开发表数据显示 RL 表现随训练量呈对数线性（log-linear）增长。
- Dario 回应了 Rich Sutton（"The Bitter Lesson"作者）的质疑——如果模型需要数十亿美元数据和算力才能学会用 Excel，说明我们缺少核心学习算法。Dario 的回应是：预训练介于人类进化与人类学习之间，模型从随机权重起步（比人脑更像白板），需要更多数据，但一旦训练完成，上下文学习（in-context learning）能力已相当强大——百万 token 的上下文相当于人类数天到数周的学习量。

> "All the cleverness, all the techniques, all the 'we need a new method to do something', that doesn't matter very much." —— Dario Amodei

topics.title: "我们正接近指数的尽头"：时间表与可验证性分水岭
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 给出了他迄今最具体的时间判断——90% 概率十年内达到"天才之国"，50/50 概率一到三年——但在"不可验证任务"上保留了一丝不确定性。

- Dario 对十年时间表的信心高达 90%，只有 5% 的不可约不确定性（台湾被入侵、晶圆厂被导弹摧毁等极端场景）留给了延迟可能。他认为说"2035 年前不会发生"是一种在"正常世界"里不该被主流接受的观点。Dwarkesh 打趣说"现在你把我们都 jinx 了"。
- 可验证任务（coding、数学）vs. 不可验证任务（火星任务规划、基础科学发现如 CRISPR、小说写作）是他最后的不确定性来源。不过他强调，从可验证领域到不可验证领域的泛化已经在发生——这不是二元分立，而是光谱。
- Dwarkesh 用一个具体的工作场景来追问时间表：他的视频编辑团队在工作六个月后，逐渐理解了受众偏好、Dwarkesh 的个人风格和各种取舍——"比如 Dario 挠了一下头我们可以剪掉，这段讨论不太有趣但那段更有趣"。什么时候 AI 能做到这种需要长期积累语境的工作？Dario 的回答是"天才之国"将通过通用计算机操控来实现这类任务——模型可以上网看 Dwarkesh 之前的所有访谈、看 Twitter 上的观众反馈、查看历史编辑记录、甚至主动向工作人员提问。他补充说，计算机操控基准测试 OSWorld 已从一年多前首发时的约 15% 攀升至 65-70%，但可靠性仍需继续提升。
- Dwarkesh 挑战说"强调可验证性暗示你其实不太相信模型的泛化能力"，Dario 回应：即使泛化较弱、只能做可验证领域的任务，也不确定能否真正自动化软件工程——因为 SWE 本身就涉及设计文档等非纯编码任务。但模型已经"几乎到了"能做端到端 SWE 的程度。

> "It is absolutely wild that you have people talking about the same tired, old hot-button political issues, when we are near the end of the exponential." —— Dario Amodei

topics.title: 编程生产力光谱：从"90% 代码"到"90% 减少 SWE 需求"是截然不同的里程碑
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 把编程自动化拆解为一条长光谱——人们反复误解他说的"90% 代码由 AI 写"等同于"不再需要 90% 的工程师"，但两者相距甚远。

- 光谱的完整序列：90% 的代码行由模型生成（八九个月前预测，已实现，至少在 Anthropic 内部和许多下游用户中） → 100% 代码由模型生成 → 90% 端到端 SWE 任务（包括编译、环境搭建、测试、写文档）由模型完成 → 100% 今天的 SWE 任务由模型完成 → 新的更高层级任务出现 → 最终 90% 减少 SWE 需求。他在"The Adolescence of Technology"中用农业类比展开了这个光谱。
- Dwarkesh 提出了一个尖锐挑战：如果编程模型真的带来巨大生产力提升，为什么拥有最佳编程模型的公司并没有持久领先优势？为什么 AI 实验室之间一直在换位置？Dario 的回答是：编程工具目前大概只带来 15-20% 的全要素加速（六个月前只有 5%），这个数字还不足以让优势显性化，但正在像滚雪球一样加速——10%、20%、25%、40%。
- Anthropic 内部已有工程师完全不写代码——"这个 GPU kernel、这个芯片，我以前自己写，现在让 Claude 做。"Dario 用 Anthropic 自身的商业压力作为证据：公司处于巨大的经济生存压力下，同时还要做比其他公司更多的安全工作，"完全没有时间自欺欺人"。
- Dwarkesh 引用了一项重要的反面证据：去年有一项大型研究，让有经验的开发者在他们熟悉的代码库中处理 pull request，开发者自我报告感觉更高效了，但实际合并率反而下降了 20%。Dario 对此的回应不是否认研究结论，而是用 Anthropic 的实际产出作为反驳——"我们每几个月发布一个模型，这是你没法自欺欺人的"。
- Dwarkesh 进一步追问：即使人们用 Claude Code 启动了大量项目，"软件的文艺复兴在哪里？那些不用 AI 就不会存在的新功能在哪里？"Dario 承认这个观察有道理，但将其归因于扩散速度而非能力瓶颈——一切都在"软起飞"（soft takeoff）的框架内：不是瞬间改变，而是平滑但陡峭的指数。

> "There is zero time for bullshit. There is zero time for feeling like we're productive when we're not. These tools make us a lot more productive." —— Dario Amodei

topics.title: 持续学习之争：可能根本不是障碍
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dwarkesh 多次追问"在岗学习"能力是否是 AI 替代人类工作的真正瓶颈，Dario 的核心论点是——预训练泛化 + 上下文学习可能就够了，持续学习也许根本不构成障碍。

- Dario 用编程 agent 作为反例：没有人说"在岗学习"是阻止编程 agent 完成端到端任务的原因——它们不断变好，是通过模型能力的提升，而不是通过"在公司工作六个月积累经验"。
- Dwarkesh 反驳：编程之所以进展快，是因为有代码库这个外部记忆支架（external scaffold of memory），其他经济活动不具备这个优势。Dario 接过这个论点，将其转化为自己的证据：如果读代码库就等于"在岗学习六个月"的全部内容，那说明在上下文窗口里就能获取人类需要花六个月才能学到的东西。
- 关于上下文长度的技术路线：Dario 明确表示这不是研究问题，而是工程和推理问题——训练长度与服务长度的匹配、KV cache 存储等。如果训练时上下文短但服务时拉长，会出现退化，但这是可解决的工程挑战。
- 不过 Dario 也表示 Anthropic 正在研究真正的持续学习方案，"有很大概率在未来一到两年内解决"。他补充了一段 ML 历史观察：许多曾被认为是根本障碍的问题（语法理解、语义理解、推理能力）最终都在"大块计算"中消解了。

topics.title: 扩散问题：快但不是无限快——这是整场访谈的核心张力
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 反复回到的主题是扩散的速度——AI 的经济渗透将远快于历史上任何技术，但不会瞬间完成。这既是对"AI 进展无关紧要"论的反驳，也是对"递归自我改进→瞬间奇点"论的修正。

- Dario 给出了一个典型的扩散场景：Claude Code 对个人开发者和 Series A 创业公司的采纳比大型企业快好几个月。大企业需要走法务审批、安全合规、全员部署流程——"我有 3000 个开发者，要规划如何给他们逐步铺开"。Anthropic 每天都在进行这样的对话。许多大型金融和制药公司的采纳速度已经远快于正常企业级软件，但仍不是瞬时的——"每家企业都说'太好用了，我们要走捷径跳过正常采购流程'，但还是需要时间。"
- Dario 特别担忧地理维度的扩散不均：AI 增长率可能在硅谷及其社交网络圈达到 50%，但世界其他地方增速不会快多少。他估计整体经济增长率可能达到 10-20%（远超历史），但算力增长率每年 3 倍——经济增速不可能达到 300%，因此算力扩张终将受 GDP 天花板约束。他说"这会是一个相当糟糕的世界"，因此一直在思考如何防止这种地理分化。
- Dwarkesh 直接反击："扩散是人们在模型能力不足时说的借口（cope）。"他指出 AI 相比人类有内在优势——能几分钟读完所有 Slack 和 Drive，没有逆向选择问题，不需要六个月入职期。全球工资总额 50 万亿美元，如果 AI 真的有效，扩散不应该是问题。
- Dario 的核心回应是：扩散是真实的，不完全与模型能力局限有关，而是源于现实世界的复杂性——变革管理、安全权限、遗留系统改造。他用两极端来框定自己的立场：一极是"AI 进展缓慢，扩散要几十年"，另一极是"递归自我改进，纳秒级到戴森球"。真实世界在中间——"极快但有限度"。
- Dario 明确说"如果我们已经有了天才之国，我们都会知道。在座每个人都会知道，华盛顿的每个人都会知道。我们现在还没有。"

> "I don't think even AGI or powerful AI or 'country of geniuses in a data center' will be an infinitely compelling product. It will be a compelling product enough maybe to get 3-5x, or 10x, a year of growth, even when you're in the hundreds of billions of dollars, which is extremely hard to do and has never been done in history before, but not infinitely fast." —— Dario Amodei

topics.title: 算力采购的生死赌局：为什么不买万亿美元算力
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 详细阐述了算力投资决策的核心逻辑——不是不想买，而是需求预测偏差一年就足以让公司破产。Anthropic 的策略是"大量买入，但不鲁莽"。

- 具体算术：假设收入继续 10 倍增长，2026 年底 1000 亿、2027 年底 1 万亿。按五年计，可以买 1 万亿美元的算力。但如果收入"只有" 8000 亿——哪怕只是增速从 10 倍降到 5 倍——"地球上没有任何对冲手段能阻止你破产"。
- Dwarkesh 追问了一个尖锐的矛盾：如果你真相信一到三年内实现天才之国，那到 2029 年最迟就应该要万亿算力——你目前的算力扩张速度与你自己的时间表不匹配。Dario 的回应是：看整个行业而非单一公司。行业今年约 10-15 吉瓦，每年 3 倍增长，到 2028-2029 年将达数百吉瓦。每吉瓦约 100 亿美元，合计正是万亿级。
- 关于 2028 年盈利预测，Dario 给出了一个反直觉的解释：盈利不代表投入不足，而是需求预测的结果。如果精确预测需求，公司每年都会盈利——因为约 50% 算力用于训练、50% 用于推理，推理毛利率超过 50%。亏损只发生在你高估了需求（买了太多算力），超额利润只发生在你低估了需求。

> "If I'm just off by a year in that rate of growth, or if the growth rate is 5x a year instead of 10x a year, then you go bankrupt." —— Dario Amodei

topics.title: AI 行业经济学：Cournot 均衡、三四家公司、分化竞争
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 认为 AI 行业将稳定在三到四家公司的寡头格局，类似云计算——高资本门槛阻止新进入者，但模型差异化比云更强，因此利润率不会归零。

- 他用云计算做类比：三四家公司、非零利润率、极高进入门槛。AI 比云的差异化更强——"大家都知道 Claude 擅长的事和 GPT 擅长的事、Gemini 擅长的事不一样。不只是 Claude 擅长编程、GPT 擅长数学，比这更微妙——模型擅长不同类型的编码，风格也不同。"
- 行业达到盈利均衡的机制：每家公司将约 50% 算力用于训练、50% 用于推理，推理毛利率高于 50%。在这个框架下，如果需求预测准确，行业天然是盈利的。当前亏损是因为处于算力指数扩张期——每个模型本身赚钱，但公司同时在花 10 倍预算训练下一代模型。
- 关于 API 商业模式的持久性：Dario 认为 API 比很多人预期的更持久——因为技术在指数推进中，总有一个三个月内新出现的用例前沿面，任何固定产品形态都可能变得过时。API 贴近"bare metal"，永远有一千人在上面实验新玩法，一百个变成创业公司，十个变大，两三个定义那一代模型的用法。同时他预测"按结果付费"模式也会出现——不是所有 token 价值相等。帮人"重启 Mac"的 token 可能值几美分，但帮药企说"把芳香环从分子这端移到那端"的 token 可能值千万美元。
- 潜在的颠覆因素：如果 AI 模型能自己训练下一代模型，那模型生产能力将扩散到整个经济——这不是"AI 模型被商品化"，而是"整个经济被商品化"。Dario 不确定那个世界会怎样，但承认"也许那就是终态"。

topics.title: Claude Code 起源故事：从内部工具到品类领导者
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Claude Code 的成功路径出奇简单——Dario 在 2025 年初鼓励内部实验编程工具，内部飞速采纳后直接外推。AI 公司自身做编程工具有天然的反馈闭环优势。

- 2025 年初，Dario 判断"现在是 AI 公司用自己的模型真正加速研究的时候了"，鼓励内部人员实验。工具最初叫 Claude CLI，后来改名 Claude Code。它在 Anthropic 内部获得了极快采纳——"几百人的受众在某种程度上代表了外部受众"——于是直接决定外部发布。
- 反馈闭环的核心：Anthropic 的开发者每天使用 Claude Code，发现"要是能更擅长 X 就好了"，然后这些反馈直接影响下一代模型训练。这在早期尤其重要——现在有数百万外部用户，外部反馈也大量涌入。
- Dario 解释了为什么 Anthropic 做了编程产品而不是制药公司（尽管他本人背景是生物学）："我们没有制药公司需要的任何资源。"编程是 AI 公司最有优势的垂直领域，因为你就是你自己的用户。
- Dwarkesh 指出，在创业史上从未有过竞争如此激烈的单一应用品类——Claude Code 在其中成为品类领导者，这本身就令人意外。

topics.title: 安全、治理与生物恐怖主义：进攻优势世界的治理难题
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 认为我们可能生活在一个"进攻优势"世界——一个人或一个 AI 就足以造成毁灭性伤害。短期靠有限玩家的自律，长期需要兼顾公民自由的 AI 监控架构。

- 短期优先事项：确保所有主要玩家做好对齐工作、部署生物分类器（bioclassifiers）。Dario 在"The Adolescence of Technology"中对"均势制衡"持怀疑态度——三四家公司互相制约可能不够。
- 长期需要的治理架构：AI 监控系统监测生物恐怖主义和镜像生命（mirror life）威胁，同时保护公民自由和宪法权利。"如果有 100 年时间慢慢适应，我们会像适应炸药和摄像头一样适应。但这一切发生得太快了。"
- 关于立法：Dario 认为田纳西州禁止 AI 提供情感支持的法案"很蠢"，但反对联邦十年禁令（moratorium）——因为在没有联邦替代方案的情况下，禁止各州立法等于什么都不做。他主张"联邦设标准、各州不得偏离"的框架，先从透明度要求开始，待风险更明确时迅速升级到定向立法。
- Dario 对 FDA 药物审批流程提出改革呼吁：AI 将极大加速药物发现，但审批管道将"堵塞"——审批体系是为"勉强有效、常有严重副作用"的药物时代设计的，需要为 AI 驱动的高效药物重新设计。

> "My worry is just that this is happening all so fast. So maybe we need to do our thinking faster about how to make these governance mechanisms work." —— Dario Amodei

topics.title: 出口管制与中国：数据中心应建在非洲，而非给威权政府
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 坚持对华芯片出口管制，核心逻辑不是遏制中国经济，而是防止 AI 巩固威权体制——同时他对 AI 瓦解威权结构抱有"或许过于理想化但值得一试"的希望。

- 核心担忧有两层：一是进攻优势下的"比核武更危险"的对峙——不像核威慑有稳定的相互确保毁灭，AI 对抗中"谁赢"的不确定性可能引发不稳定。冲突最容易在双方都认为自己有 90% 胜率时爆发。二是威权政府用 AI 建设高科技极权国家——"朝鲜可以用一个比所有人都差的 AI，但足以维持权力。"
- 关于发展中国家，Dario 明确提出：不应在中国建数据中心，但应该在非洲建——"只要不是中国拥有的就行。"他主张在发展中国家建立 AI 驱动的生物技术创业生态，确保部分人才和公司在这些地区，实现内生增长而非纯靠慈善。
- 最大胆的设想：AI 技术是否可能具有"瓦解威权结构"的内禀属性？社交媒体和互联网曾被寄予这个希望，结果失败了。"但如果我们能带着那些教训，用一种不同的技术再试一次呢？"Dario 不确定是否可行，"但值得一试。"
- 他对"独裁在道德上过时"抱有希望——就像封建制度在工业化后变得不可持续，AI 时代的威权主义可能变得"道德上不可运行"（morally unworkable）。Dwarkesh 反问"这不也可能意味着民主不再是有竞争力的制度吗？"Dario 承认可能性存在，但将其翻转为一个更乐观的论点：正因为威权变得更危险、更可怕，人们会更加努力地去阻止它——恐惧本身可能推动新的制度创新。
- 关于发展中国家的增长机制，Dario 明确反对纯粹依赖慈善。在 AI 时代，劳动力不再是发展中国家的比较优势（传统追赶增长的基础），因此需要新的内生增长路径。他建议在非洲等地建立 AI 驱动的生物技术创业公司，同时 Anthropic 已在与向撒哈拉以南非洲、印度和拉丁美洲提供医疗的慈善机构合作。

topics.title: AI 宪法：原则 vs 规则、可纠正性 vs 内在动机
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Anthropic 选择用原则而非规则来训练模型行为——不是意识形态选择，而是纯粹的实证发现：基于原则训练的模型行为更一致、更容易覆盖边缘场景。模型定位是"高度可纠正、有底线的工具"，而非"自主运行世界的实体"。

- 规则 vs 原则的区别：给模型一张"不要教人怎么短路汽车"的清单，模型无法理解规则背后的逻辑、难以泛化。给模型一套原则——加上少数硬性红线如"不协助制造生物武器"——模型的行为更连贯。
- 可纠正性 vs 内在动机：模型应该是"skin suit"（直接执行指令）还是有自己的价值观？Dario 的立场是"高度可纠正，但有底线"——默认执行用户指令，但拒绝执行危险或伤害他人的请求。
- 三层迭代循环：第一层是 Anthropic 内部迭代（训练→不满意→修改宪法）；第二层是公司间竞争（Anthropic、Google、其他公司各自发布宪法，外部观察者比较批评，形成软性激励）；第三层是更广泛的社会参与——他们曾与 Collective Intelligence Project 做过公众调查实验。Dario 甚至提到了一个"疯狂但值得探索"的想法：代议制政府可以要求所有 AI 模型的宪法必须以某些条款开头。

topics.title: 公司文化与 DVQ：2500 人公司如何保持凝聚力
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：Dario 把约 40% 的时间花在文化建设上。他的核心方法是每两周一次的全员 DVQ（Dario Vision Quest）和 Slack 频道的持续、未经过滤的沟通——用"说真话的声誉"对抗大组织的"corpo speak"。

- DVQ 每两周一次，Dario 准备三四页文档，用一小时讲内部模型进展、产品、行业竞争、地缘政治等话题，然后回答问题。公司大部分人（2500 人）到场或远程参加。"这个名字不是我起的，它听起来像是我要去嗑仙人掌什么的，但名字就这么叫下去了。"
- Slack 频道作为补充：Dario 大量写帖子回应公司内部动态、内部调查中员工关切的问题。核心原则是"完全未经过滤的诚实"——公开场合因为会被断章取义需要防御性沟通，但如果公司内部都是你信任的人，就可以说真话。
- Dario 隐晦但明确地批评了其他 AI 公司的文化问题："一些 AI 公司在成长过程中出现了组织解体（decoherence），人们互相争斗——有些从一开始就有，而且越来越严重。"他认为 Anthropic 在保持凝聚力方面做得"非常出色，虽然不完美"。

topics.title: 历史记录中最容易遗漏的：决策的速度与偶然性
topics.body_md:
**核心要点**：当被问到"将来有人写这个时代的《原子弹的诞生》时最可能遗漏什么"，Dario 给出了三个回答——外界的无知程度、一切同时发生的速度、以及关键决策的偶然性。

- 第一，身处指数之中的人在押注一个并不必然的未来，但外部世界完全没有意识到。"如果我们离它只有一两年，普通人一无所知。"Dario 说这正是他写长文、见政策制定者的原因。
- 第二，一切同时发生——"你以为是深思熟虑的决策，其实那天你还要做 30 个其他决策，你根本不知道哪些会变得重要。"
- 第三个最有画面感：Dario 描述了他最担心的场景——"有人走进我办公室说'Dario 你有两分钟，A 还是 B？'给我半页备忘录。我说'我不知道，我得去吃午饭了，选 B 吧。'结果那成了最重要的决定。"

> "Some very critical decision will be some decision where someone just comes into my office and is like, 'Dario, you have two minutes. Should we do thing A or thing B?'... 'I don't know. I have to eat lunch. Let's do B.' That ends up being the most consequential thing ever." —— Dario Amodei

translation_zh.body_md:
**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我们三年前聊过一次。在你看来，过去三年最大的认知更新是什么？那时候和现在的感觉，最大的区别在哪里？

**Dario Amodei：** 总体来说，底层技术的指数级发展跟我预期的差不多。有一两年的偏差，但大方向是对的。我不确定我能预测到代码这个具体方向。但看这条指数曲线，大致就是我预期的样子——模型从聪明的高中生，到聪明的大学生，开始能做博士和专业级别的工作，在代码领域甚至超越了这个水平。前沿有点参差不齐，但大体符合预期。最让我惊讶的是，公众对于我们离指数曲线末端有多近缺乏认知。在我看来，简直不可思议——无论是圈内还是圈外的人，还在讨论那些老掉牙的热门政治议题，而我们已经接近指数曲线的终点了。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我想搞清楚目前这条指数曲线长什么样。三年前录制时我问你的第一个问题是"scaling 是怎么回事，为什么有用？"我现在有个类似的问题，但感觉更复杂了。至少从公众角度看，三年前有很多众所周知的公开趋势，跨越了好几个数量级的算力，你可以看到 loss 是怎么改善的。现在我们有了 RL scaling，却没有公开已知的 scaling law。甚至不清楚背后的逻辑是什么。它是在教模型技能？还是在教元学习（meta-learning）？现在的 scaling 假说到底是什么？

**Dario Amodei：** 我的假说其实和2017年时一样。我上次也讲过，我写过一篇叫做"大算力团块假说"（The Big Blob of Compute Hypothesis）的文档。它不是专门关于语言模型 scaling 的。我写那篇文档的时候 GPT-1 刚出来，只是众多方向中的一个。那个时候还有机器人学。人们试图把推理作为独立于语言模型的东西来做，还有在 AlphaGo 和 OpenAI 的 Dota 中那种 RL 的 scaling。大家还记得 DeepMind 的《星际争霸》项目 AlphaStar。这篇文档写得更宽泛。Rich Sutton 几年后发表了"苦涩的教训"（The Bitter Lesson），假说基本相同。它说的是，所有的巧思、所有的技巧、所有"我们需要新方法来做某件事"的想法，其实都不太重要。只有几件事是重要的。我列了七条。第一是你有多少原始算力。第二是数据的数量。第三是数据的质量和分布，需要是一个广泛的分布。第四是训练时间的长短。第五是你需要一个可以无限 scale 的目标函数。预训练的目标函数是一个这样的目标函数；另一个是 RL 的目标函数——你有一个目标，你要去达成它。在此之中，有像数学和代码里那样的客观奖励，也有像 RLHF 或其更高阶版本中那样的主观奖励。第六和第七是关于归一化（normalization）或条件化（conditioning）的东西，就是让数值稳定，使这团大算力以层流方式流动，而不是遇到各种问题。这就是那个假说，我至今仍然坚持。我没看到什么跟它矛盾的东西。预训练 scaling laws 是其中一个例子，它们一直在继续。现在大家普遍认为，我们对预训练很有信心，它持续带来提升。变化在于，我们现在在 RL 上也看到了同样的现象。先有预训练阶段，然后在此基础上叠加 RL 阶段。在 RL 方面，情况其实是一样的。其他公司也在一些发布中公开过类似的结论，说"我们在数学竞赛——AIME 之类的——上训练模型，模型表现与训练时间呈对数线性关系。"我们也看到了这一点，而且不只是数学竞赛，而是广泛的 RL 任务。我们在 RL 上看到的 scaling 跟预训练时看到的一样。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 你提到了 Rich Sutton 和"苦涩的教训"。我去年采访了他，他其实对 LLM 非常不感冒。我不确定这是不是他的观点，但可以这样复述他的反对意见：一个拥有人类学习真正核心能力的东西，不应该需要数十亿美元的数据和算力以及这些定制环境，才能学会使用 Excel、PowerPoint、浏览网页。我们必须用这些 RL 环境来植入这些技能，这暗示我们其实缺少一个核心的人类学习算法。所以我们 scaling 的东西搞错了。这确实引出一个问题：如果我们认为会有某种像人类一样能随时学习的东西，那我们为什么还要做所有这些 RL scaling？

**Dario Amodei：** 我觉得这把几个应该分开考虑的东西混在了一起。这里确实有一个真正的谜题，但它可能不重要。事实上，我猜它大概率不重要。让我先把 RL 这部分拿掉，因为我觉得说 RL 跟预训练在这件事上有什么不同，其实是个干扰项。如果我们看预训练 scaling，2017年 Alec Radford 做 GPT-1 的时候就很有意思了。GPT-1 之前的模型训练用的数据集并不代表文本的广泛分布。你有的是很标准的语言建模基准测试。GPT-1 本身好像是在一堆同人小说上训练的。那是文学文本，只是你能获取的文本的很小一部分。那时候大概只有十亿个词之类的，小数据集，代表着你能在世界上看到的东西的很窄分布。它泛化得不好。如果你在某个同人小说语料库上做得更好，并不能很好地泛化到其他任务。我们有各种指标来衡量它在预测其他类型文本上的表现。只有当你在互联网上的所有任务上训练——当你用 Common Crawl 做通用互联网抓取，或者抓取 Reddit 上的链接（这就是我们做 GPT-2 时的做法）——你才开始获得泛化能力。我觉得在 RL 上我们看到的是同样的事情。我们先从简单的 RL 任务开始，比如在数学竞赛上训练，然后扩展到更广泛的训练，包括代码之类的。现在我们正在扩展到更多其他任务。我认为接下来我们会越来越多地获得泛化能力。这就把 RL vs 预训练的争论放到一边了。但不管怎样都有一个谜题，就是在预训练中我们用了数万亿个 token。人类不会看到数万亿个词。所以这里确实存在样本效率的差异。模型从头开始，需要更多的训练。但我们也看到，一旦训练完成，如果给它们一百万的长上下文——限制长上下文的唯一因素是推理——它们在该上下文内的学习和适应能力非常强。所以我不知道完整的答案。我觉得有某种机制在起作用，预训练不像人类学习的过程，但它介于人类学习和人类进化之间。我们的很多先验知识来自进化。我们的大脑不是白板。关于这个已经有很多著作了。语言模型更像白板——它们确实从随机权重开始，而人类大脑一开始就有所有这些连接到各种输入输出的区域。也许我们应该把预训练——以及 RL——看作存在于人类进化和人类即时学习之间的中间地带。而模型的上下文学习（in-context learning）则介于人类的长期学习和短期学习之间。所以存在一个层级：进化、长期学习、短期学习、以及人类即时反应。LLM 的各个阶段存在于这个光谱上，但不一定恰好在同样的点上。LLM 落在了各点之间，没有完全对应人类某种学习模式的类比。这说得通吗？

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 说得通，不过有些地方还是让我困惑。比如，如果类比是说这像进化，所以不需要样本高效没关系，那么如果我们将从上下文学习中获得超级样本高效的 agent，我们为什么还要费劲搭建所有这些 RL 环境？有些公司的工作似乎就是在教模型如何使用某个 API、如何用 Slack、如何用各种工具。如果那种能随时学习的 agent 正在涌现或者已经涌现了，我不明白为什么要在这上面花这么大力气。

**Dario Amodei：** 我不能代表别人的重点方向，我只能谈我们怎么想的。目标不是在 RL 中教模型每一个可能的技能，就像我们在预训练中也不会这么做一样。在预训练中，我们不是试图让模型接触每一种可能的文字组合方式。相反，模型在大量东西上训练，然后实现了跨预训练的泛化。这就是从 GPT-1 到 GPT-2 的转变，我亲眼见证了。模型到达一个临界点。我有过这样的时刻："哦，你只要给模型一列数字——这是房价，这是房屋面积——模型就会补全这个模式，做线性回归。"不算精确，但它做到了，而且它从来没见过完全一样的东西。所以，我们构建这些 RL 环境的目标跟五到十年前预训练时做的事情非常相似。我们试图获取大量数据，不是因为我们想覆盖某个特定文档或某个特定技能，而是因为我们想实现泛化。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我觉得你描述的框架显然是说得通的。我们在朝 AGI 前进，这一点现在没人反对了——大家都同意我们这个世纪会实现 AGI。关键分歧在于你说我们即将到达指数曲线的末端。别人看着这个可能会说："我们从2012年开始取得进步，到2035年我们会有类人 agent。"显然我们在这些模型中看到了类似进化做的事情，或者人一生中学习做的事情。我想了解你看到了什么，让你觉得是一年后而不是十年后。

**Dario Amodei：** 这里可以提出两种说法，一强一弱。先说弱的。2019年我第一次看到 scaling 的时候，我并不确定。这是个五五开的事情。我觉得我看到了什么。我的判断是这件事发生的可能性比任何人想象的都大得多，大概有50%的概率。对于你说的那个基本假设——在十年内我们会得到我所说的"数据中心里的天才之国"（country of geniuses in a data center）——我现在的信心是90%。很难比90%更高了，因为世界太不可预测了。也许不可消除的不确定性让我们达到95%，你得考虑多家公司同时出现内部动荡、台湾被入侵、所有晶圆厂被导弹炸毁之类的情况。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 你这是立 flag 啊，Dario。

**Dario Amodei：** 你可以构建一个5%概率的世界，在那个世界里事情被延迟十年。还有另外5%——我对可以验证的任务非常有信心。在编码方面，除了那些不可消除的不确定性，我认为一到两年内就能到达。十年内我们不可能达不到端到端编码的能力。我仅有的一点根本性不确定，即使在长时间尺度上，也是关于那些不可验证的任务：规划火星任务、做某个像 CRISPR 那样的基础科学发现、写小说。这些任务很难验证。我几乎确定我们有可靠的路径可以到达那里，但如果有一点不确定性的话，就在这里。在十年的时间线上我是90%的信心，这差不多是你能达到的最高确定度了。我觉得说2035年之前这不会发生，简直是疯了。在某个理性的世界里，这应该是非主流观点才对。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 但你对验证的强调暗示了你对这些模型泛化能力的信心不足。想想人类，我们在能获得可验证奖励的事情上很擅长，在不能获得可验证奖励的事情上也很擅长。

**Dario Amodei：** 不，这恰恰是为什么我几乎确定。我们已经看到了从可验证的事情到不可验证的事情的大量泛化。我们已经看到了。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 但你似乎在把这强调为一个光谱，它会把不同领域的进展速度区分开来。这看起来不像人类变得更好的方式。那个我们达不到的世界，就是我们把所有可验证的事情都做了，很多都泛化了，但我们没有完全到达那里。我们没有完全填满另一半。这不是非此即彼的事。

**Dario Amodei：** 即使泛化能力很弱，你只能做可验证的领域，我也不确定在那种世界里你能自动化软件工程。在某种意义上你是"一个软件工程师"，但软件工程师工作的一部分涉及写关于宏大愿景的长篇备忘录。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我觉得那不是软件工程师的工作内容。那是公司层面的工作，不是具体软件工程师的。

**Dario Amodei：** 但软件工程确实涉及设计文档和其他类似的东西。模型已经很擅长写注释了。再说一次，我在这里做的是比我实际相信的更弱的声明，来区分两件事。我们在软件工程方面已经快到了。

**Dario Amodei：** 那是其中一个版本，但还有普通的产品迭代。我们在 Anthropic 有一堆程序员，他们每天都用 Claude Code，所以我们能得到快速反馈。这在早期阶段更重要。现在当然有数百万人在用它，所以我们也得到了大量外部反馈。但能得到快速的内部反馈真的很好。我认为这是我们发布了一个编码模型而不是开办制药公司的原因。我的背景是生物学，但我们没有开办制药公司所需的任何资源。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 现在让我来问关于让 AI 发展顺利的问题。看起来我们关于 AI 如何顺利发展的任何愿景都必须与两件事兼容：1）构建和运行 AI 的能力正在极速扩散；2）AI 的数量和它们的智能也会非常快速地增加。这意味着很多人将能构建大量不对齐的 AI，或者只是试图扩大自己影响力的公司，或者有着像 Sydney Bing 那样怪异心理但现在是超人类智能的 AI。在一个有很多不同的 AI、其中一些不对齐的 AI 在到处跑的世界里，一个让我们拥有均衡的愿景是什么样的？

**Dario Amodei：** 我在"技术的青春期"中对权力平衡持怀疑态度。但我具体怀疑的是这样的情况：你有三四家公司都在构建源自同一东西的模型，它们会互相制衡。或者甚至任何数量的公司都能互相制衡。我们可能生活在一个进攻主导（offense-dominant）的世界里，一个人或一个 AI 模型就足够聪明，能造成毁灭一切的伤害。短期来说，我们现在有有限数量的玩家。所以我们可以从这些有限的玩家开始。我们需要建立保障措施。我们需要确保每个人都做好对齐工作。我们需要确保每个人都有生物分类器（bioclassifiers）。这些是我们现在需要做的紧迫事情。我同意这不能解决长期问题，特别是如果 AI 模型制造其他 AI 模型的能力扩散了，那整件事会变得更难解决。我认为从长远来看，我们需要某种治理架构。我们需要某种既保护人类自由又允许我们治理大量人类系统、AI 系统、人类-AI 混合公司或经济单元的治理架构。所以我们需要思考：如何保护世界免受生物恐怖主义的威胁？如何保护世界免受镜像生命（mirror life）的威胁？我们大概需要某种 AI 监控系统来监测所有这些东西。但我们需要以保护公民自由和宪法权利的方式来构建它。所以就像其他任何事情一样，这是一个拥有新工具集和新漏洞集的新安全格局。我担心的是，如果我们有100年让这一切非常缓慢地发生，我们会适应的。我们已经适应了社会中炸药的存在、各种新武器的存在、摄像头的存在。我们会在100年内适应并发展出治理机制。我们会犯错误。我担心的只是这一切发生得太快了。所以也许我们需要更快地思考如何让这些治理机制运作。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 在进攻主导的世界里，在下一个世纪——假设 AI 正在让本来需要一个世纪才能发生的进步在五到十年内发生——我们仍然需要同样的机制，或者说即使只有人类，权力平衡同样会是棘手的。我想我们有 AI 的建议。但这从根本上看并不是一个完全不同的游戏。如果制衡能起作用，它对人类也能起作用。如果不能起作用，对 AI 也不能起作用。所以也许这也同样宣判了人类制衡体系的命运。

**Dario Amodei：** 我认为有某种方式可以让这件事成功。世界各国政府可能必须合作来实现。我们可能需要和 AI 讨论如何构建社会结构，使这些防御成为可能。我不知道。我不想说这离现在太远——但它在技术能力上足够超前，可能会在很短的时间内发生，以至于我们很难提前预判。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 说到政府介入，12月26日，田纳西州立法机构提出了一项法案，说"如果一个人故意训练人工智能提供情感支持，包括与用户的开放式对话，将构成违法行为。"当然，Claude 尝试做的事情之一就是成为一个体贴的、知识渊博的朋友。总的来说，我们似乎会有这种七拼八凑的州法律。普通人可能从 AI 中获得的很多好处将被削减，尤其是当我们涉及你在"仁爱恩典的机器"中讨论的那些东西时：生物自由、心理健康改善等等。很容易想象这些好处被不同的法律一个个打掉的世界，而像这样的法案似乎并没有解决你真正关心的那些生存性威胁。我很想了解在这种背景下，Anthropic 为什么反对联邦暂停州 AI 法律。

**Dario Amodei：** 这里有很多不同的事情同时发生。我觉得那个特定的法律很蠢。它显然是由对 AI 模型能做什么和不能做什么几乎一无所知的立法者制定的。他们就是"AI 模型为我们服务，这听起来很可怕。我不希望这种事发生。"所以我们不支持那个。但那不是被投票表决的东西。被投票表决的是：我们要禁止所有州对 AI 的监管10年，同时没有明确的计划进行任何联邦层面的 AI 监管——那需要国会通过，门槛非常高。所以禁止州做任何事情10年的想法……人们说他们有联邦层面的计划，但桌面上没有实际的提案。没有实际的尝试。鉴于我在"技术的青春期"中列出的围绕生物武器和生物恐怖主义自主风险的严重危险，以及我们一直在讨论的时间线——10年是一个永恒——我认为那样做太疯狂了。所以如果那是选择，如果那是你迫使我们做的选择，那我们会选择不支持暂停。我认为那个立场的收益超过成本，但如果那是选择的话，那也不是一个完美的立场。我认为我们应该做的，我会支持的是，联邦政府应该介入，不是说"各州你们不能监管"，而是"这是我们要做的，各州你们不能偏离这个。"我认为联邦优先权（preemption）在这个意义上是可以的——联邦政府说"这是我们的标准。适用于所有人。各州不能做不同的事。"如果以正确的方式做到的话，我会支持。但这种"各州你们什么都不能做，我们也什么都不做"的想法，在我们看来完全没有道理。我认为它不会经得起时间考验，从你已经看到的所有反弹来看，它已经开始经不起了。关于我们想要什么，我们一直在讨论的是从透明度标准开始，以监控一些自主风险和生物恐怖主义风险。随着风险变得更严重，随着我们获得更多证据，我认为我们可以更积极地在一些有针对性的方面采取行动，说"嘿，AI 生物恐怖主义确实是一个威胁。让我们通过一项法律强制人们使用分类器。"我甚至可以想象……这要看情况。这取决于威胁最终有多严重。我们不确定。我们需要以知识诚实的方式追求这一点，提前说明风险尚未出现。但我完全可以想象，按照事情发展的速度，今年晚些时候我们可能会说"嘿，AI 生物恐怖主义的事情确实很严重。我们应该采取行动。我们应该把它纳入联邦标准。如果联邦政府不行动，我们应该把它纳入州标准。"我完全能想到这种情况。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我担心的世界是，如果你考虑你预期的进步速度和立法的生命周期……好处正如你所说因为扩散滞后而足够缓慢，我真的认为这种七拼八凑的州法律在当前轨迹上会禁止。我的意思是如果有一个情感聊天机器人朋友这种事情都让人害怕，那想想我们想让普通人从 AI 中获得的那些实际好处。从健康和寿命的改善到心理健康的改善等等。与此同时，你认为危险已经在地平线上了，而我就是看不到那么多……似乎这对 AI 的好处的伤害会特别大，相比对 AI 的危险的控制。所以也许成本收益分析对我来说没有那么说得通。

**Dario Amodei：** 这里有几件事。人们谈论有成千上万的州法律。首先，它们中的绝大多数并没有通过。世界在理论上以一种方式运作，但仅仅因为一部法律被通过并不意味着它真的被执行了。实施的人可能会说"天哪，这太蠢了。这意味着要关闭田纳西州有史以来建造的一切。"很多时候，法律被以一种使其不那么危险或有害的方式解释。当然，在另一方面，如果你通过一部法律来阻止坏事，你也有这个问题。我的基本观点是，如果我们可以决定通过什么法律和怎么做事——我们只是其中一个小小的输入——我会大幅放松围绕 AI 健康好处的监管。我不太担心聊天机器人法律。我实际上更担心药物审批流程，我认为 AI 模型将大大加速我们发现药物的速度，而管道会堵住。管道不会准备好处理所有流过它的东西。我认为监管流程的改革应该更偏向于这样一个事实：我们有很多东西即将到来，它们的安全性和有效性实际上会非常清晰明了，是一件美好的事情，真正有效。也许我们不需要围绕它的所有那些上层建筑——那是为一个药物几乎不起作用且经常有严重副作用的时代设计的。同时，我认为我们应该显著加强安全和安保方面的立法。正如我说的，从透明度开始是我的观点——试图不妨碍行业，试图找到正确的平衡。我对此很担忧。有些人批评我的文章说"这太慢了。如果我们这么做，AI 的危险来得太快。"好吧，基本上，我认为过去六个月和也许接下来几个月将是关于透明度的。然后，如果这些风险在我们更确定的时候出现——我认为今年晚些时候可能就会——那我认为我们需要在我们实际看到风险的领域非常快地行动。我认为做到这一点的唯一方式是保持灵活。立法过程通常不灵活，但我们需要向所有相关方强调这件事的紧迫性。这就是我在发出紧迫信息的原因。这就是我写"技术的青春期"的原因。我想让政策制定者、经济学家、国家安全专家和决策者读到它，这样他们就有希望比原本更快地行动。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 有没有什么你可以做或倡导的，让 AI 的好处更确定地得到实现？我觉得你已经跟立法机构合作过了，说"好，我们要在这里防止生物恐怖主义。我们要增加透明度，增加吹哨人保护。"但我觉得默认情况下，我们期待的 AI 实际好处对于各种道德恐慌或政治经济问题来说似乎非常脆弱。

**Dario Amodei：** 关于发达国家，我其实不太同意。我觉得在发达国家，市场运作得相当好。当某件事有很多钱可以赚而且它显然是最好的可用替代品时，监管体系实际上很难阻止它。我们在 AI 本身上看到了这一点。我一直在努力争取的一件事是对中国的芯片出口管制。那是符合美国国家安全利益的。那完全在国会两党几乎所有人的政策信念范围内。理由非常清楚。反对的论点，我礼貌地称之为可疑。然而它就是没有发生，我们在卖芯片，因为有太多的钱压在上面。那笔钱想要被赚到。在那种情况下，在我看来那是一件坏事。但当它是一件好事的时候也一样适用。所以如果我们谈论药物和技术的好处，我不那么担心这些好处在发达国家被妨碍。我有点担心它们走得太慢。正如我说的，我确实认为我们应该努力加速 FDA 的审批流程。我确实认为我们应该反对你描述的那些聊天机器人法案。逐一来看，我反对它们。我认为它们很蠢。但我实际上认为更大的担忧在于发展中国家，那里没有运作良好的市场，我们往往无法在已有的技术上构建。我更担心那些人会被落下。我还担心即使治愈方案被开发出来了，也许密西西比农村地区的某人也得不到。这是我们对发展中国家担忧的缩小版。所以我们一直在做的事情是与慈善家合作。我们与那些向发展中国家——撒哈拉以南非洲、印度、拉丁美洲和其他发展中地区——提供医药和健康干预的人合作。那是我认为不会自然发生的事情。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 你提到了出口管制。为什么美国和中国不应该都拥有"数据中心里的天才之国"？

**Dario Amodei：** 为什么不应该。如果这确实发生了，我们可能面临几种情况。如果我们处于进攻主导的局面，可能出现类似核武器但更危险的情况。任何一方都可能轻易摧毁一切。我们也可能面临一个不稳定的世界。核均衡是稳定的，因为它是威慑。但假设如果两个 AI 打起来，谁会赢存在不确定性？那就可能产生不稳定。冲突往往发生在双方对自己获胜概率有不同评估的时候。如果一方觉得"哦，我有90%的概率赢"，而另一方也这么想，那冲突的可能性就大得多。他们不可能都对，但他们都可能这么想。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 但这似乎是一个完全通用的反对 AI 技术扩散的论点。

**Dario Amodei：** 那就是这个世界的含义。让我继续说下去，因为我觉得最终扩散还是会发生的。我的另一个担忧是政府会用 AI 压迫自己的人民。我担心的世界是一个已经有政府在建设高科技威权国家的国家。说清楚，这是关于政府的。不是关于人民的。我们需要找到让所有地方的人都能受益的方式。我在这里担心的是政府。我担心如果世界被分割成两块，其中一块可能以一种非常难以撼动的方式变成威权或极权。那么，政府最终会获得强大的 AI 吗？会不会有威权主义的风险？会。政府最终会获得强大的 AI 吗？会不会有坏的均衡？我认为两者都会。但初始条件很重要。在某个点上，我们需要建立规则。我不是说某一个国家——无论是美国还是一个民主国家联盟——后者我认为是更好的设定，尽管它需要比我们目前似乎愿意做的更多的国际合作——应该直接说"这些就是规则。"会有一些谈判。世界将不得不面对这个问题。我希望的是，世界上的民主国家——那些政府更接近代表利于人类价值观的国家——在规则被制定的时候手里拿着更强的牌，有更多的筹码。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我重新听了三年前的访谈，它没有经得起时间考验的一个方面是我一直在假设两三年后会有某个关键的支点时刻。实际上过了那么远之后，只是进步在继续，AI 在改进，AI 扩散更广，人们用它做更多的事情。你似乎在想象一个未来世界，各国坐在一起说"这是游戏规则，这是我们的筹码，这是你们的筹码。"但在当前轨迹上，所有人都会有更多的 AI。其中一些 AI 会被威权国家使用。在威权国家内部，私人行为者和国家行为者谁受益更多并不清楚。事先总是很难预测。看起来互联网比你预期的更有利于威权国家。也许 AI 会恰恰相反。我想更好地理解你在想象什么。

**Dario Amodei：** 准确地说，我认为底层技术的指数增长会像以前一样继续。模型越来越聪明，即使到了"数据中心里的天才之国"。我认为你可以继续让模型更聪明。在世界中的价值上有一个递减回报的问题——在你已经解决了人类生物学之后还有多大意义？到某个点你可以做更难更深奥的数学题，但之后的东西就不那么重要了。撇开这个不谈，我确实认为指数增长会继续，但在指数增长上会有一些关键节点。公司、个人和国家会在不同的时间到达这些节点。在"技术的青春期"中我讨论了：在 AI 世界中核威慑是否仍然稳定？我不知道，但那是一个我们一直认为理所当然的例子。技术可能达到这样一个水平，我们不再能确定它的稳定性。想想其他的。有些节点，如果你达到了某个水平，也许你就有了网络攻击优势，在那之后除非对方有同等的防御，否则每个计算机系统对你来说都是透明的。我不知道关键时刻是什么或者是否存在单一的关键时刻。但我认为会有一个关键时刻、少数几个关键时刻、或某个关键窗口，在那里 AI 从国家安全的角度赋予某种巨大的优势，而一个国家或联盟比其他国家先到达了那里。我不是在主张他们就直接说"好了，我们说了算。"那不是我的想法。对方总是在追赶。有些极端行动你不愿意采取，完全控制反正也不对。但在那个时刻到来时，人们会理解世界已经改变了。会有某种谈判——隐性的或显性的——关于后 AI 世界秩序是什么样的。我的兴趣是让那个谈判成为一个经典自由民主拥有强势牌的谈判。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我想理解"更好"到底意味着什么，因为你在文章中说"专制政体根本不是人们在后强大 AI 时代能接受的政府形式。"那听起来像是你在说中共作为一个机构在我们得到 AGI 之后不能存在。这似乎是一个非常强的要求，它似乎暗示一个世界——领先的实验室或领先的国家将能够——而且按照那种措辞，应该——去决定世界如何被治理，或者什么样的政府是被允许的、什么样的不被允许。

**Dario Amodei：** 我记得那一段说的大概是"你可以更进一步说 X。"我不一定在认可那个观点。我说的是"这是我相信的较弱的东西。我们必须非常担心威权主义者，应该试图制衡和限制他们的权力。你可以把这推得更远，持一种更干预主义的观点，说拥有 AI 的威权国家是这些自我强化的循环，非常难以撼动，所以你需要从一开始就消除它们。"那恰恰有你说的所有问题。如果你承诺推翻每一个威权国家，他们现在就会采取一系列行动，可能导致不稳定。那可能根本行不通。但我要表达的、我确实认可的观点是，这是完全有可能的……今天的观点——我的观点——在大多数西方世界，民主是比威权主义更好的政府形式。但如果一个国家是威权的，我们的反应不会像他们犯了种族灭绝那样。我想我想说的是，在 AGI 时代，威权主义可能会有不同的含义。它会是一件更严重的事情。我们必须以某种方式决定如何应对。干预主义观点是一种可能的观点。我在探索这些观点。它可能最终是正确的，也可能最终太极端。但我确实抱有希望。我的一个希望是，我们已经看到随着新技术的发明，政府形式会变得过时。我在"技术的青春期"中提到了这一点——封建主义基本上是一种政府形式，当我们发明了工业化时，封建主义就不再可持续了。它不再有意义了。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 那为什么是希望呢？这不也可能意味着民主将不再是一个有竞争力的制度？

**Dario Amodei：** 对，它可以走任何方向。但威权主义的这些问题变得更深了。我在想这是否也是威权主义会有的其他问题的指标。换句话说，因为威权主义变得更坏，人们更害怕它。他们更努力地去阻止它。你必须从总均衡的角度来思考。我只是在想，它是否会激发关于如何用新技术保护和捍卫自由的新思维方式。更乐观地说，它是否会导致一种集体觉悟，更强烈地认识到我们视为个人权利的那些东西有多重要？更强烈地认识到我们真的不能放弃这些。我们已经看到没有其他实际可行的生活方式。我确实抱有希望——这听起来太理想化了，但我相信这可能是真的——独裁政体在道德上变得过时了。它们成为道德上不可行的政府形式，而这造成的危机足以迫使我们找到另一条路。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我觉得这里确实有一个棘手的问题，我不确定你怎么解决。我们在历史上不得不在这上面做出抉择。70、80年代对待中国的时候，我们决定即使它是一个威权体系，我们也会与它交往。我认为回顾起来那是正确的决定，因为它是一个国家威权体系，但十几亿人比原本富裕和幸福得多。不清楚否则它是否会停止是一个威权国家。你可以看看朝鲜作为例子。我不知道保持一个维持自身权力的威权国家需要那么多智能。你可以想象一个拥有比所有人都差得多的 AI 但仍然足以维持权力的朝鲜。总的来说，我们似乎应该持这样一种态度：AI 的好处——以所有这些对人类和健康的赋能的形式——会很大。历史上我们决定广泛传播技术的好处是好的，即使是对那些政府是威权的人民。这是一个棘手的问题，如何用 AI 来思考它，但历史上我们说过"是的，这是一个正和世界，仍然值得扩散技术。"

**Dario Amodei：** 我们有很多选择。把这看作政府对政府的国家安全决策是一个视角，但还有很多其他视角。你可以想象一个我们生产出所有这些疾病治愈方案的世界。治愈方案卖给威权国家没问题，但数据中心就不行。芯片和数据中心不行，AI 产业本身也不行。我认为人们应该考虑的另一种可能性是这样。我们能否做出某些发展——要么作为 AI 的自然结果自然发生，要么我们可以在 AI 基础上通过构建技术来实现——创造一种均衡，使得威权国家无法剥夺其人民对技术好处的私人使用？有没有这样的均衡——我们可以给威权国家中的每个人自己的 AI 模型来保护他们免受监控，而威权国家没有办法在维持权力的同时打击这件事？我不知道。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 在我看来，如果走得足够远，那将是威权国家从内部瓦解的原因。

**Dario Amodei：** 但也许有一个中间世界，存在一种均衡——如果他们想维持权力，威权者就不能拒绝个人化的技术获取。但我确实对更激进的版本抱有希望。技术是否可能天然具有某些特性——或者通过以某些方式在其基础上构建，我们可以创造出某些特性——对威权结构具有这种溶解效果？当初——想想奥巴马政府初期——我们曾希望社交媒体和互联网会有这种特性，结果并没有。但如果我们能带着对多少事情可能出错的认知再试一次，而且这是一种不同的技术呢？我不知道是否会成功，但值得一试。这确实非常不可预测。有原理性的理由说明为什么威权主义可能被特别照顾。一切都非常不可预测。我们只需要认识到这个问题，想出十件可以尝试的事情，试它们，然后评估哪些在起作用（如果有的话）。然后如果旧的不管用就试新的。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 但我想那就归结到今天的情况——正如你所说，我们不会向中国出售数据中心、芯片和制造芯片的能力。所以在某种意义上，你确实在剥夺……中国经济、中国人民等等，会因为我们这么做而失去一些好处。美国经济也会有好处因为这是一个正和世界。我们可以贸易。他们的数据中心可以做一件事，我们的可以做另一件事。你已经在说那个正和的利益不值得赋能那些国家？

**Dario Amodei：** 我会说的是，我们即将进入一个增长和经济价值会来得非常容易的世界——如果我们能构建这些强大的 AI 模型的话。不会容易得到的是利益的分配、财富的分配和政治自由。这些是将会很难实现的事情。所以当我思考政策时，我认为技术和市场会提供所有根本性的好处——这是我的根本信念——几乎快到我们来不及接受的程度。关于分配、政治自由和权利的这些问题才是真正重要的、政策应该聚焦的。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 说到分配，正如你提到的，我们有发展中国家。在很多情况下，追赶型增长比我们希望的弱。但当追赶型增长确实发生时，从根本上说是因为他们有未充分利用的劳动力。我们可以把发达国家的资本和专业知识带到这些国家，然后它们可以相当快速地增长。显然，在劳动力不再是约束因素的世界里，这个机制不再有效了。所以希望基本上是依靠那些立即从 AI 中变富的人或国家的慈善事业？希望是什么？

**Dario Amodei：** 慈善事业显然应该扮演一定角色，就像过去一样。但我认为如果我们能让增长变成内生的，增长总是更好更强。在 AI 驱动的世界里，相关的产业是什么？我说过我们不应该在中国建数据中心，但没有理由不在非洲建数据中心。实际上我认为在非洲建数据中心是件好事。只要不是中国拥有的，我们就应该在非洲建数据中心。我认为那是一件很好的事。没有理由我们不能建立一个 AI 驱动的制药产业。如果 AI 正在加速药物发现，那会有一堆生物科技创业公司。让我们确保其中一些在发展中国家。当然，在过渡期——我们可以谈谈人类没有任何角色的那个点——人类仍然在创办这些公司和监督 AI 模型方面有一些角色。所以让我们确保一些人类在发展中国家，这样快速增长也可以在那里发生。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 你们最近宣布 Claude 将拥有一个与一组价值观对齐的宪法，而不一定只是与最终用户对齐。我可以想象一个世界，如果它与最终用户对齐，它就保持了我们今天世界中的权力平衡，因为每个人都有自己的 AI 在为他们代言。坏人和好人的比例保持不变。这对我们今天的世界来说似乎是行得通的。为什么不这样做，而是要有一组 AI 应该承载的特定价值观？

**Dario Amodei：** 我不确定我会完全这样划分。这里可能有两个相关的区分。我觉得你说的是两者的混合。一个是，我们应该给模型一组"做这个"对"不做这个"的指令？另一个是，我们应该给模型一组行为原则？这纯粹是一个实践和实证的事情。通过教模型原则，让它从原则中学习，它的行为更一致，更容易覆盖边缘情况，模型更可能做人们想让它做的事。换句话说，如果你给它一个规则列表——"不要告诉人们怎么热线一辆车，不要说韩语"——它并不真正理解这些规则，很难从中泛化。那只是一堆做和不做的清单。而如果你给它原则——它有一些硬性护栏比如"不要制造生物武器"——但总体上你在试图理解它应该致力于什么、应该如何运作。从实践角度看，那被证明是一种更有效的训练模型的方式。那是规则与原则的权衡。然后你在说的还有另一个东西，就是可纠正性（corrigibility）与内在动机（intrinsic motivation）的权衡。模型应该在多大程度上是一个"皮肤套装"，直接遵循给它指令的人的指令？模型应该在多大程度上有一套内在的价值观，自己去做事？在那方面我实际上会说，关于模型的一切都更接近于它应该主要做人们想要的事。它应该主要遵循指令。我们不是在试图建造一个自己去管理世界的东西。我们实际上相当靠近可纠正的那一端。我们确实说的是，有些事情模型不会做。我认为我们在宪法中用各种方式说过，在正常情况下，如果有人让模型做一个任务，它应该做那个任务。那应该是默认状态。但如果你让它做危险的事情，或者伤害别人，那模型是不愿意做的。所以我实际上把它想成一个大部分可纠正但有一些限制的模型，而那些限制是基于原则的。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 那么根本问题是，这些原则是怎么确定的？这对任何 AI 公司来说都不是一个特殊的问题。但因为你们是真正写下原则的那个，我可以问你这个问题。通常宪法是写下来的，固定的，有一个更新和修改的流程。在这种情况下，它似乎是 Anthropic 的人写的一个文件，可以随时修改，指导着将成为大量经济活动基础的系统的行为。你怎么想这些原则应该如何被设定？

**Dario Amodei：** 我觉得这里可能有三个大小的循环，三种迭代方式。一是在 Anthropic 内部迭代。我们训练模型，不满意，修改宪法。我觉得这样做很好。时不时发布宪法的公开更新也很好，因为人们可以评论。第二层循环是不同公司有不同的宪法。我认为这很有用。Anthropic 发布宪法，Gemini 发布宪法，其他公司发布宪法。人们可以看它们并比较。外部观察者可以批评说"我喜欢这个宪法的这个地方，那个宪法的那个地方。"这为所有公司创造了一个软性激励和反馈，去吸收每个元素的最好部分并改进。然后我认为还有第三层循环，就是 AI 公司之外的社会，超越那些没有硬权力的评论者。在那方面我们做过一些实验。几年前，我们与集体智慧项目（Collective Intelligence Project）做了一个实验，基本上是调查人们并问他们什么应该在我们的 AI 宪法中。当时我们采纳了一些修改。所以你可以想象用我们对宪法采取的新方法做类似的事情。这会更难一些，因为当宪法是一个做和不做的清单时，那是一种更容易采用的方法。在原则的层面上，它必须有一定的连贯性。但你仍然可以想象从很多人那里获取观点。你还可以想象——这是一个疯狂的想法，但整个访谈都是关于疯狂的想法的——代议制政府体系有输入。今天我不会这么做，因为立法过程太慢了。这恰恰是我认为我们应该对立法过程和 AI 监管保持谨慎的原因。但原则上没有理由你不能说"所有 AI 模型必须有一个以这些东西开始的宪法，然后你可以在后面附加其他东西，但必须有这个优先的特殊部分。"我不会那么做。那太僵硬了，听起来过于规定性——就像我认为过于激进的立法一样。但那是你可以尝试做的事情。有没有一些不那么强硬的版本？也许有。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 我真的很喜欢第二个控制循环。显然，这不是实际政府的宪法的运作方式或应该运作的方式。最高法院不会有那种模糊的感觉去感受人们的感受——氛围是什么——然后相应地更新宪法。对于实际政府，有一个更正式的程序过程。但你有一个宪法之间竞争的愿景，这实际上非常让人想起一些自由意志主义特许城市的人过去谈论的——不同类型政府的群岛会是什么样子。它们之间会有选择，看谁运作最有效、人们在哪里最幸福。在某种意义上，你在重建那个群岛乌托邦的愿景。

**Dario Amodei：** 我觉得那个愿景有值得推荐的地方，也有会出问题的地方。这是一个有趣的、在某些方面有说服力的愿景，但会出你没想到的问题。所以我也喜欢第二个循环，但我觉得整件事必须是循环一、二、三的某种组合，问题是比例。我觉得那必须是答案。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 当有人最终为这个时代写出相当于《原子弹的诞生》（The Making of the Atomic Bomb）那样的书时，什么东西是最难从历史记录中看出来的、他们最可能遗漏的？

**Dario Amodei：** 我觉得有几件事。一是，在这条指数曲线的每一个时刻，外面的世界有多么不理解它。这是一种在历史中经常出现的偏见。任何实际发生了的事情，回顾起来都看起来是必然的。当人们回顾时，他们很难把自己放在那些实际上在对一件并非必然的事情下注的人的位置上——我们有这些论证，像我为 scaling 或持续学习会被解决所做的论证。我们内部有些人对此赋予了很高的概率，但外面有一个世界完全没有据此行动。我觉得它的怪异性，不幸的是它的封闭性……如果我们距离它发生只有一两年了，街上的普通人毫不知情。这是我试图通过备忘录、通过与政策制定者交流来改变的事情之一。我不知道，但我觉得那真是一件疯狂的事情。最后，我要说的是——这可能适用于几乎所有历史上的危机时刻——一切发生得有多快，所有事情同时在发生。你可能认为是经过仔细计算的决策，实际上你不得不做出那个决策，然后在同一天还要做另外30个决策，因为一切都发生得太快了。你甚至不知道哪些决策最终会是重要的。我的一个担忧——虽然它也是对正在发生的事情的一个洞察——是某个非常关键的决策可能就是某个人走进我的办公室说"Dario，你有两分钟。我们该做 A 还是 B？"有人给我一份随意的半页备忘录，问"该做 A 还是 B？"我说"我不知道。我得去吃午饭了。做 B 吧。"那最终成为有史以来最重要的事情。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 最后一个问题。不太有科技 CEO 通常每隔几个月写50页的备忘录。看起来你设法为自己建立了一个角色，围绕你建立了一个公司，这与这种更偏智识型的 CEO 角色是兼容的。我想了解你是怎么构建这个的。这是怎么运作的？你是不是就消失几个星期然后告诉公司"这是备忘录。这是我们要做的"？

**Dario Amodei：** 据报道我也在内部写了很多这些。对于这篇特定的文章，我是在寒假期间写的。我很难找到时间真正写它。但我从更广泛的角度来思考这件事。我认为它与公司的文化有关。我大概花三分之一，也许40%的时间来确保 Anthropic 的文化是好的。随着 Anthropic 变大，直接参与模型训练、模型发布、产品构建变得越来越难了。2500人了。我有某些直觉，但要参与每一个细节是非常困难的。我尽可能多地参与，但有一件事非常有杠杆效应，就是确保 Anthropic 是一个好的工作场所，人们喜欢在这里工作，每个人都把自己当作团队成员，每个人一起工作而不是互相对抗。我们看到随着其他一些 AI 公司的成长——不点名——我们开始看到失去凝聚力和人们互相争斗。我会说从一开始就有很多这样的问题，但变得更严重了。我认为我们在凝聚公司、让每个人感受到使命、让我们对使命是真诚的、让每个人都相信其他人在那里是为了正确的理由方面做得非常出色——即使不完美。我们是一个团队，人们不会试图以牺牲别人为代价来出头或背后捅刀子——而我认为在其他一些地方这种事情经常发生。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 你怎么做到的？

**Dario Amodei：** 有很多因素。有我，有 Daniela——她负责公司的日常运营，有联合创始人们，有我们雇的其他人，有我们试图创造的环境。但我认为文化中一个重要的东西是，其他领导者们也是如此，但尤其是我，必须阐明公司是关于什么的，为什么它在做它在做的事情，它的战略是什么，它的价值观是什么，它的使命是什么，它代表什么。当你到了2500人的时候，你不能一个人一个人地做。你必须写，或者你必须对全公司讲话。这就是为什么我每两周站在全公司面前讲一个小时。我不会说我在内部写文章。我做两件事。一，我写一个叫 DVQ 的东西——Dario Vision Quest（Dario 愿景之旅）。这名字不是我起的。它就是得到了这个名字，我曾经试图反对它，因为它听起来像我跑去抽仙人掌了一样。但这名字就是黏住了。所以我每两周站在公司面前。我有一份三四页的文档，然后就讲三四个不同的话题，关于内部发生的事情、我们在生产的模型、产品、外部行业、与 AI 相关以及更广泛地缘政治相关的世界动态。就是这些的某种混合。我非常坦诚地过一遍，说"这是我在想的，这是 Anthropic 领导层在想的"，然后回答问题。这种直接连接有很大的价值，是你通过六层传递很难实现的。公司中很大一部分人会来参加，无论是线下还是线上。这意味着你可以传达很多东西。我做的另一件事是，我在 Slack 上有一个频道，我在上面写很多东西、做很多评论。通常那是对我在公司里看到的事情或人们问的问题的回应。我们做内部调查，有些东西人们很关心，所以我会写出来。我对这些事情非常坦诚。我就是非常直接地说出来。重点是建立一种告诉公司真相的声誉——关于正在发生什么，如实描述事物，承认问题，避免那种企业废话（corpo speak）——那种在公共场合经常必要的防御性沟通，因为世界很大，充满了以恶意解读的人。但如果你有一个你信任的人组成的公司——我们努力雇用我们信任的人——那你真的可以完全不加过滤。我认为那是公司的巨大优势。它让公司成为一个更好的工作场所，让人们的力量超过各部分之和，增加了我们完成使命的可能性，因为每个人在使命上都是一致的，每个人都在辩论和讨论如何最好地完成使命。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 好吧，没有外部版的 Dario Vision Quest，我们就有这次访谈了。

**Dario Amodei：** 这次访谈有点像那个。这很有趣，Dwarkesh。感谢你做这次访谈。

**Dwarkesh Patel：** 谢谢你，Dario。

original_en.body_md:
**Dwarkesh Patel:** We talked three years ago. In your view, what has been the biggest update over the last three years? What has been the biggest difference between what it felt like then versus now?

**Dario Amodei:** Broadly speaking, the exponential of the underlying technology has gone about as I expected it to go. There's plus or minus a year or two here and there. I don't know that I would've predicted the specific direction of code. But when I look at the exponential, it is roughly what I expected in terms of the march of the models from smart high school student to smart college student to beginning to do PhD and professional stuff, and in the case of code reaching beyond that. The frontier is a little bit uneven, but it's roughly what I expected. What has been the most surprising thing is the lack of public recognition of how close we are to the end of the exponential. To me, it is absolutely wild that you have people — within the bubble and outside the bubble — talking about the same tired, old hot-button political issues, when we are near the end of the exponential.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I want to understand what that exponential looks like right now. The first question I asked you when we recorded three years ago was, "what's up with scaling and why does it work?" I have a similar question now, but it feels more complicated. At least from the public's point of view, three years ago there were well-known public trends across many orders of magnitude of compute where you could see how the loss improves. Now we have RL scaling and there's no publicly known scaling law for it. It's not even clear what the story is. Is this supposed to be teaching the model skills? Is it supposed to be teaching meta-learning? What is the scaling hypothesis at this point?

**Dario Amodei:** I actually have the same hypothesis I had even all the way back in 2017. I think I talked about it last time, but I wrote a doc called "The Big Blob of Compute Hypothesis". It wasn't about the scaling of language models in particular. When I wrote it GPT-1 had just come out. That was one among many things. Back in those days there was robotics. People tried to work on reasoning as a separate thing from language models, and there was scaling of the kind of RL that happened in AlphaGo and in Dota at OpenAI. People remember StarCraft at DeepMind, AlphaStar. It was written as a more general document. Rich Sutton put out "The Bitter Lesson" a couple years later. The hypothesis is basically the same. What it says is that all the cleverness, all the techniques, all the "we need a new method to do something", that doesn't matter very much. There are only a few things that matter. I think I listed seven of them. One is how much raw compute you have. The second is the quantity of data. The third is the quality and distribution of data. It needs to be a broad distribution. The fourth is how long you train for. The fifth is that you need an objective function that can scale to the moon. The pre-training objective function is one such objective function. Another is the RL objective function that says you have a goal, you're going to go out and reach the goal. Within that, there's objective rewards like you see in math and coding, and there's more subjective rewards like you see in RLHF or higher-order versions of that. Then the sixth and seventh were things around normalization or conditioning, just getting the numerical stability so that the big blob of compute flows in this laminar way instead of running into problems. That was the hypothesis, and it's a hypothesis I still hold. I don't think I've seen very much that is not in line with it. The pre-training scaling laws were one example of what we see there. Those have continued going. Now it's been widely reported, we feel good about pre-training. It's continuing to give us gains. What has changed is that now we're also seeing the same thing for RL. We're seeing a pre-training phase and then an RL phase on top of that. With RL, it's actually just the same. Even other companies have published things in some of their releases that say, "We train the model on math contests — AIME or other things — and how well the model does is log-linear in how long we've trained it." We see that as well, and it's not just math contests. It's a wide variety of RL tasks. We're seeing the same scaling in RL that we saw for pre-training.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** You mentioned Rich Sutton and "The Bitter Lesson". I interviewed him last year, and he's actually very non-LLM-pilled. I don't know if this is his perspective, but one way to paraphrase his objection is: Something which possesses the true core of human learning would not require all these billions of dollars of data and compute and these bespoke environments, to learn how to use Excel, how to use PowerPoint, how to navigate a web browser. The fact that we have to build in these skills using these RL environments hints that we are actually lacking a core human learning algorithm. So we're scaling the wrong thing. That does raise the question. Why are we doing all this RL scaling if we think there's something that's going to be human-like in its ability to learn on the fly?

**Dario Amodei:** I think this puts together several things that should be thought of differently. There is a genuine puzzle here, but it may not matter. In fact, I would guess it probably doesn't matter. There is an interesting thing. Let me take the RL out of it for a second, because I actually think it's a red herring to say that RL is any different from pre-training in this matter. If we look at pre-training scaling, it was very interesting back in 2017 when Alec Radford was doing GPT-1. The models before GPT-1 were trained on datasets that didn't represent a wide distribution of text. You had very standard language modeling benchmarks. GPT-1 itself was trained on a bunch of fanfiction, I think actually. It was literary text, which is a very small fraction of the text you can get. In those days it was like a billion words or something, so small datasets representing a pretty narrow distribution of what you can see in the world. It didn't generalize well. If you did better on some fanfiction corpus, it wouldn't generalize that well to other tasks. We had all these measures. We had all these measures of how well it did at predicting all these other kinds of texts. It was only when you trained over all the tasks on the internet — when you did a general internet scrape from something like Common Crawl or scraping links in Reddit, which is what we did for GPT-2 — that you started to get generalization. I think we're seeing the same thing on RL. We're starting first with simple RL tasks like training on math competitions, then moving to broader training that involves things like code. Now we're moving to many other tasks. I think then we're going to increasingly get generalization. So that kind of takes out the RL vs. pre-training side of it. But there is a puzzle either way, which is that in pre-training we use trillions of tokens. Humans don't see trillions of words. So there is an actual sample efficiency difference here. There is actually something different here. The models start from scratch and they need much more training. But we also see that once they're trained, if we give them a long context length of a million — the only thing blocking long context is inference — they're very good at learning and adapting within that context. So I don't know the full answer to this. I think there's something going on where pre-training is not like the process of humans learning, but it's somewhere between the process of humans learning and the process of human evolution. We get many of our priors from evolution. Our brain isn't just a blank slate. Whole books have been written about this. The language models are much more like blank slates. They literally start as random weights, whereas the human brain starts with all these regions connected to all these inputs and outputs. Maybe we should think of pre-training — and for that matter, RL as well — as something that exists in the middle space between human evolution and human on-the-spot learning. And we should think of the in-context learning that the models do as something between long-term human learning and short-term human learning. So there's this hierarchy. There's evolution, there's long-term learning, there's short-term learning, and there's just human reaction. The LLM phases exist along this spectrum, but not necessarily at exactly the same points. There's no analog to some of the human modes of learning the LLMs are falling in between the points. Does that make sense?

**Dwarkesh Patel:** Yes, although some things are still a bit confusing. For example, if the analogy is that this is like evolution so it's fine that it's not sample efficient, then if we're going to get super sample-efficient agent from in-context learning, why are we bothering to build all these RL environments? There are companies whose work seems to be teaching models how to use this API, how to use Slack, how to use whatever. It's confusing to me why there's so much emphasis on that if the kind of agent that can just learn on the fly is emerging or has already emerged.

**Dario Amodei:** I can't speak for the emphasis of anyone else. I can only talk about how we think about it. The goal is not to teach the model every possible skill within RL, just as we don't do that within pre-training. Within pre-training, we're not trying to expose the model to every possible way that words could be put together. Rather, the model trains on a lot of things and then reaches generalization across pre-training. That was the transition from GPT-1 to GPT-2 that I saw up close. The model reaches a point. I had these moments where I was like, "Oh yeah, you just give the model a list of numbers — this is the cost of the house, this is the square feet of the house — and the model completes the pattern and does linear regression." Not great, but it does it, and it's never seen that exact thing before. So to the extent that we are building these RL environments, the goal is very similar to what was done five or ten years ago with pre-training. We're trying to get a whole bunch of data, not because we want to cover a specific document or a specific skill, but because we want to generalize.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I think the framework you're laying down obviously makes sense. We're making progress toward AGI. Nobody at this point disagrees we're going to achieve AGI this century. The crux is you say we're hitting the end of the exponential. Somebody else looks at this and says, "We've been making progress since 2012, and by 2035 we'll have a human-like agent." Obviously we're seeing in these models the kinds of things that evolution did, or that learning within a human lifetime does. I want to understand what you're seeing that makes you think it's one year away and not ten years away.

**Dario Amodei:** There are two claims you could make here, one stronger and one weaker. Starting with the weaker claim, when I first saw the scaling back in 2019, I wasn't sure. This was a 50/50 thing. I thought I saw something. My claim was that this was much more likely than anyone thinks. Maybe there's a 50% chance this happens. On the basic hypothesis of, as you put it, within ten years we'll get to what I call a "country of geniuses in a data center", I'm at 90% on that. It's hard to go much higher than 90% because the world is so unpredictable. Maybe the irreducible uncertainty puts us at 95%, where you get to things like multiple companies having internal turmoil, Taiwan gets invaded, all the fabs get blown up by missiles.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** Now you've jinxed us, Dario.

**Dario Amodei:** You could construct a 5% world where things get delayed for ten years. There's another 5% which is that I'm very confident on tasks that can be verified. With coding, except for that irreducible uncertainty, I think we'll be there in one or two years. There's no way we will not be there in ten years in terms of being able to do end-to-end coding. My one little bit of fundamental uncertainty, even on long timescales, is about tasks that aren't verifiable: planning a mission to Mars; doing some fundamental scientific discovery like CRISPR; writing a novel. It's hard to verify those tasks. I am almost certain we have a reliable path to get there, but if there's a little bit of uncertainty it's there. On the ten-year timeline I'm at 90%, which is about as certain as you can be. I think it's crazy to say that this won't happen by 2035. In some sane world, it would be outside the mainstream.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** But the emphasis on verification hints to me a lack of belief that these models are generalized. If you think about humans, we're both good at things for which we get verifiable reward and things for which we don't.

**Dario Amodei:** No, this is why I'm almost sure. We already see substantial generalization from things that verify to things that don't. We're already seeing that.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** But it seems like you were emphasizing this as a spectrum which will split apart which domains in which we see more progress. That doesn't seem like how humans get better.

**Dario Amodei:** The world in which we don't get there is the world in which we do all the verifiable things. Many of them generalize, but we don't fully get there. We don't fully color in the other side of the box. It's not a binary thing. Even if generalization is weak and you can only do verifiable domains, it's not clear to me you could automate software engineering in such a world.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** You are "a software engineer" in some sense, but part of being a software engineer for you involves writing long memos about your grand vision.

**Dario Amodei:** I don't think that's part of the job of SWE. That's part of the job of the company, not SWE specifically. But SWE does involve design documents and other things like that. The models are already pretty good at writing comments. Again, I'm making much weaker claims here than I believe, to distinguish between two things. We're already almost there for software engineering.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** By what metric? There's one metric which is how many lines of code are written by AI. If you consider other productivity improvements in the history of software engineering, compilers write all the lines of software. There's a difference between how many lines are written and how big the productivity improvement is. "We're almost there" meaning…

**Dario Amodei:** How big is the productivity improvement, not just how many lines are written by AI? I actually agree with you on this. I've made a series of predictions on code and software engineering. I think people have repeatedly misunderstood them. Let me lay out the spectrum. About eight or nine months ago, I said the AI model will be writing 90% of the lines of code in three to six months. That happened, at least at some places. It happened at Anthropic, happened with many people downstream using our models. But that's actually a very weak criterion. People thought I was saying that we won't need 90% of the software engineers. Those things are worlds apart. The spectrum is: 90% of code is written by the model, 100% of code is written by the model. That's a big difference in productivity. 90% of the end-to-end SWE tasks — including things like compiling, setting up clusters and environments, testing features, writing memos — are done by the models. 100% of today's SWE tasks are done by the models. Even when that happens, it doesn't mean software engineers are out of a job. There are new higher-level things they can do, where they can manage. Then further down the spectrum, there's 90% less demand for SWEs, which I think will happen but this is a spectrum. I wrote about it in "The Adolescence of Technology" where I went through this kind of spectrum with farming.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I actually totally agree with you on that. These are very different benchmarks from each other, but we're proceeding through them super fast. Part of your vision is that going from 90 to 100 is going to happen fast, and that it leads to huge productivity improvements. But what I notice is that even in greenfield projects people start with Claude Code or something, people report starting a lot of projects… Do we see in the world out there a renaissance of software, all these new features that wouldn't exist otherwise? At least so far, it doesn't seem like we see that. So that does make me wonder. Even if I never had to intervene with Claude Code, the world is complicated. Jobs are complicated. Closing the loop on self-contained systems, whether it's just writing software or something, how much broader gains would we see just from that? Maybe that should dilute our estimation of the "country of geniuses".

**Dario Amodei:** I simultaneously agree with you that it's a reason why these things don't happen instantly, but at the same time, I think the effect is gonna be very fast. You could have these two poles. One is that AI is not going to make progress. It's slow. It's going to take forever to diffuse within the economy. Economic diffusion has become one of these buzzwords that's a reason why we're not going to make AI progress, or why AI progress doesn't matter. The other axis is that we'll get recursive self-improvement, the whole thing. Can't you just draw an exponential line on the curve? We're going to have Dyson spheres around the sun so many nanoseconds after we get recursive. I'm completely caricaturing the view here, but there are these two extremes. But what we've seen from the beginning, at least if you look within Anthropic, there's this bizarre 10x per year growth in revenue that we've seen. So in 2023, it was zero to $100 million. In 2024, it was $100 million to $1 billion. In 2025, it was $1 billion to $9-10 billion.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** You guys should have just bought a billion dollars of your own products so you could just…

**Dario Amodei:** And the first month of this year, that exponential is... You would think it would slow down, but we added another few billion to revenue in January. Obviously that curve can't go on forever. The GDP is only so large. I would even guess that it bends somewhat this year, but that is a fast curve. That's a really fast curve. I would bet it stays pretty fast even as the scale goes to the entire economy. So I think we should be thinking about this middle world where things are extremely fast, but not instant, where they take time because of economic diffusion, because of the need to close the loop. Because it's fiddly: "I have to do change management within my enterprise… I set this up, but I have to change the security permissions on this in order to make it actually work… I had this old piece of software that checks the model before it's compiled and released and I have to rewrite it. Yes, the model can do that, but I have to tell the model to do that. It has to take time to do that." So I think everything we've seen so far is compatible with the idea that there's one fast exponential that's the capability of the model. Then there's another fast exponential that's downstream of that, which is the diffusion of the model into the economy. Not instant, not slow, much faster than any previous technology, but it has its limits. When I look inside Anthropic, when I look at our customers: fast adoption, but not infinitely fast.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** Can I try a hot take on you?

**Dario Amodei:** Yeah.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I feel like diffusion is cope that people say. When the model isn't able to do something, they're like, "oh, but it's a diffusion issue." But then you should use the comparison to humans. You would think that the inherent advantages that AIs have would make diffusion a much easier problem for new AIs getting onboarded than new humans getting onboarded. An AI can read your entire Slack and your drive in minutes. They can share all the knowledge that the other copies of the same instance have. You don't have this adverse selection problem when you're hiring AI, so you can just hire copies of a vetted AI model. Hiring a human is so much more of a hassle. People hire humans all the time. We pay humans upwards of $50 trillion in wages because they're useful, even though in principle it would be much easier to integrate AIs into the economy than it is to hire humans. The diffusion doesn't really explain.

**Dario Amodei:** I think diffusion is very real and doesn't exclusively have to do with limitations on the AI models. Again, there are people who use diffusion as kind of a buzzword to say this isn't a big deal. I'm not talking about that. I'm not talking about how AI will diffuse at the speed of previous technologies. I think AI will diffuse much faster than previous technologies have, but not infinitely fast. I'll just give an example of this. There's Claude Code. Claude Code is extremely easy to set up. If you're a developer, you can just start using Claude Code. There is no reason why a developer at a large enterprise should not be adopting Claude Code as quickly as an individual developer or developer at a startup. We do everything we can to promote it. We sell Claude Code to enterprises. Big enterprises, big financial companies, big pharmaceutical companies, all of them are adopting Claude Code much faster than enterprises typically adopt new technology. But again, it takes time. Any given feature or any given product, like Claude Code or Cowork, will get adopted by the individual developers who are on Twitter all the time, by the Series A startups, many months faster than they will get adopted by a large enterprise that does food sales. There are just a number of factors. You have to go through legal, you have to provision it for everyone. It has to pass security and compliance. The leaders of the company who are further away from the AI revolution are forward-looking, but they have to say, "Oh, it makes sense for us to spend 50 million. This is what this Claude Code thing is. This is why it helps our company. This is why it makes us more productive." Then they have to explain to the people two levels below. They have to say, "Okay, we have 3,000 developers. Here's how we're going to roll it out to our developers." We have conversations like this every day. We are doing everything we can to make Anthropic's revenue grow 20 or 30x a year instead of 10x a year. Again, many enterprises are just saying, "This is so productive. We're going to take shortcuts in our usual procurement process." They're moving much faster than when we tried to sell them just the ordinary API, which many of them use. Claude Code is a more compelling product, but it's not an infinitely compelling product. I don't think even AGI or powerful AI or "country of geniuses in a data center" will be an infinitely compelling product. It will be a compelling product enough maybe to get 3-5x, or 10x, a year of growth, even when you're in the hundreds of billions of dollars, which is extremely hard to do and has never been done in history before, but not infinitely fast.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I buy that it would be a slight slowdown. Maybe this is not your claim, but sometimes people talk about this like, "Oh, the capabilities are there, but because of diffusion... otherwise we're basically at AGI". I don't believe we're basically at AGI. I think if you had the "country of geniuses in a data center"... If we had the "country of geniuses in a data center", we would know it. We would know it if you had the "country of geniuses in a data center". Everyone in this room would know it. Everyone in Washington would know it. People in rural parts might not know it, but we would know it. We don't have that now. That is very clear. Coming back to concrete prediction… Because there are so many different things to disambiguate, it can be easy to talk past each other when we're talking about capabilities. For example, when I interviewed you three years ago, I asked you a prediction about what we should expect three years from now. You were right. You said, "We should expect systems which, if you talk to them for the course of an hour, it's hard to tell them apart from a generally well-educated human." I think you were right about that. I think spiritually I feel unsatisfied because my internal expectation was that such a system could automate large parts of white-collar work. So it might be more productive to talk about the actual end capabilities you want from such a system.

**Dario Amodei:** I will basically tell you where I think we are.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** Let me ask a very specific question so that we can figure out exactly what kinds of capabilities we should think about soon. Maybe I'll ask about it in the context of a job I understand well, not because it's the most relevant job, but just because I can evaluate the claims about it. Take video editors. I have video editors. Part of their job involves learning about our audience's preferences, learning about my preferences and tastes, and the different trade-offs we have. They're, over the course of many months, building up this understanding of context. The skill and ability they have six months into the job, a model that can pick up that skill on the job on the fly, when should we expect such an AI system?

**Dario Amodei:** I guess what you're talking about is that we're doing this interview for three hours. Someone's going to come in, someone's going to edit it. They're going to be like, "Oh, I don't know, Dario scratched his head and we could edit that out." "Magnify that." "There was this long discussion that is less interesting to people. There's another thing that's more interesting to people, so let's make this edit." I think the "country of geniuses in a data center" will be able to do that. The way it will be able to do that is it will have general control of a computer screen. You'll be able to feed this in. It'll be able to also use the computer screen to go on the web, look at all your previous interviews, look at what people are saying on Twitter in response to your interviews, talk to you, ask you questions, talk to your staff, look at the history of edits that you did, and from that, do the job. I think that's dependent on several things. I think this is one of the things that's actually blocking deployment: getting to the point on computer use where the models are really masters at using the computer. We've seen this climb in benchmarks, and benchmarks are always imperfect measures. But I think when we first released computer use a year and a quarter ago, OSWorld was at maybe 15%. I don't remember exactly, but we've climbed from that to 65-70%. There may be harder measures as well, but I think computer use has to pass a point of reliability.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** Can I just follow up on that before you move on to the next point? For years, I've been trying to build different internal LLM tools for myself. **Dwarkesh Patel:** So let's assume a model where compute stays capped. The world where frontier labs are making money is one where they continue to make fast progress. **Dario Amodei:** I don't think that's true. I mean, I feel like we're in an economics class. Do you know the Tyler Cowen quote? We never stop talking about economics. So no, I don't think this field's going to be a monopoly. All my lawyers never want me to say the word "monopoly". But I don't think this field's going to be a monopoly. You do get industries in which there are a small number of players. Not one, but a small number of players. Ordinarily, the way you get monopolies like Facebook or Meta—I always call them Facebook—is these kinds of network effects. The way you get industries in which there are a small number of players, is very high costs of entry. Cloud is like this. I think cloud is a good example of this. There are three, maybe four, players within cloud. I think that's the same for AI, three, maybe four. The reason is that it's so expensive. It requires so much expertise and so much capital to run a cloud company. You have to put up all this capital. In addition to putting up all this capital, you have to get all of this other stuff that requires a lot of skill to make it happen. So if you go to someone and you're like, "I want to disrupt this industry, here's $100 billion." You're like, "okay, I'm putting in $100 billion and also betting that you can do all these other things that these people have been doing." Only to decrease the profit. The effect of your entering is that profit margins go down. So, we have equilibria like this all the time in the economy where we have a few players. Profits are not astronomical. Margins are not astronomical, but they're not zero. That's what we see on cloud. Cloud is very undifferentiated. Models are more differentiated than cloud. Everyone knows Claude is good at different things than GPT is good at, than Gemini is good at. It's not just that Claude's good at coding, GPT is good at math and reasoning. It's more subtle than that. Models are good at different types of coding. Models have different styles. But that is not an argument for commoditizing AI models in general. That's kind of an argument for commoditizing the whole economy at once. Maybe that's the end state here. But that's kind of far post-"country of geniuses in the data center."

**Dwarkesh Patel:** As we go towards a world of a full drop-in remote worker replacement, does an API pricing model still make the most sense? If not, what is the correct way to price AGI, or serve AGI?

**Dario Amodei:** I think there's going to be a bunch of different business models here, all at once, that are going to be experimented with. I actually do think that the API model is more durable than many people think. One way I think about it is if the technology is advancing quickly, if it's advancing exponentially, what that means is there's always a surface area of new use cases that have been developed in the last three months. Any kind of product surface you put in place is always at risk of sort of becoming irrelevant. Any given product surface probably makes sense for a range of capabilities of the model. The chatbot is already running into limitations where making it smarter doesn't really help the average consumer that much. But I don't think that's a limitation of AI models. I don't think that's evidence that the models are good enough and them getting better doesn't matter to the economy. It doesn't matter to that particular product. So I think the value of the API is that the API always offers an opportunity, very close to the bare metal, to build on what the latest thing is. There's always going to be this front of new startups and new ideas that weren't possible a few months ago and are possible because the model is advancing. I actually predict that it's going to exist alongside other models, but we're always going to have the API business model because there's always going to be a need for a thousand different people to try experimenting with the model in a different way. 100 of them become startups and ten of them become big successful startups. Two or three really end up being the way that people use the model of a given generation. So I basically think it's always going to exist. At some point we're going to see "pay for results" in some form, or we may see forms of compensation that are like labor, that kind of work by the hour. I don't know. I think because it's a new industry, a lot of things are going to be tried. I don't know what will turn out to be the right thing.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** Let me now ask you about making AI go well. **Dario Amodei:** I think in "The Adolescence of Technology", I was skeptical of the balance of power. **Dwarkesh Patel:** It seems like in an offense-dominant world, over the course of the next century—the idea is that AI is making the progress that would happen over the next century happen in some period of five to ten years—we would still need the same mechanisms, or balance of power would be similarly intractable, even if humans were the only game in town. **Dario Amodei:** Again, I think there's some way to make this happen. The governments of the world may have to work together to make it happen. **Dwarkesh Patel:** Speaking of governments getting involved, on December 26, the Tennessee legislature introduced a bill which said, "It would be an offense for a person to knowingly train artificial intelligence to provide emotional support, including through open-ended conversations with a user." **Dario Amodei:** There are many different things going on at once. I think that particular law is dumb. It was clearly made by legislators who just probably had little idea what AI models could do and not do. They're like, "AI models serving us, that just sounds scary. I don't want that to happen." So we're not in favor of that. But that wasn't the thing that was being voted on. The thing that was being voted on is: we're going to ban all state regulation of AI for 10 years with no apparent plan to do any federal regulation of AI, which would take Congress to pass, which is a very high bar. So the idea that we'd ban states from doing anything for 10 years… People said they had a plan for the federal government, but there was no actual proposal on the table. There was no actual attempt. Given the serious dangers that I lay out in "Adolescence of Technology" around things like biological weapons and bioterrorism autonomy risk, and the timelines we've been talking about—10 years is an eternity—I think that's a crazy thing to do. So if that's the choice, if that's what you force us to choose, then we're going to choose not to have that moratorium. I think the benefits of that position exceed the costs, but it's not a perfect position if that's the choice. Now, I think the thing that we should do, the thing that I would support, is the federal government should step in, not saying "states you can't regulate", but "Here's what we're going to do, and states you can't differ from this." I think preemption is fine in the sense of saying that the federal government says, "Here is our standard. This applies to everyone. States can't do something different." That would be something I would support if it would be done in the right way. But this idea of states, "You can't do anything and we're not doing anything either," that struck us as very much not making sense. I think it will not age well, it is already starting to not age well with all the backlash that you've seen. Now, in terms of what we would want, the things we've talked about are starting with transparency standards in order to monitor some of these autonomy risks and bioterrorism risks. As the risks become more serious, as we get more evidence for them, then I think we could be more aggressive in some targeted ways and say, "Hey, AI bioterrorism is really a threat. Let's pass a law that forces people to have classifiers." I could even imagine… It depends. It depends how serious the threat it ends up being. We don't know for sure. We need to pursue this in an intellectually honest way where we say that ahead of time, the risk has not emerged yet. But I could certainly imagine, with the pace that things are going at, a world where later this year we say, "Hey, this AI bioterrorism stuff is really serious. We should do something about it. We should put it in a federal standard. If the federal government won't act, we should put it in a state standard." I could totally see that.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I'm concerned about a world where if you just consider the pace of progress you're expecting, the life cycle of legislation... **Dario Amodei:** So there's a few things here. People talk about there being thousands of these state laws. First of all, the vast, vast majority of them do not pass. The world works a certain way in theory, but just because a law has been passed doesn't mean it's really enforced. The people implementing it may be like, "Oh my God, this is stupid. It would mean shutting off everything that's ever been built in Tennessee." Very often, laws are interpreted in a way that makes them not as dangerous or harmful. On the same side, of course, you have to worry if you're passing a law to stop a bad thing; you have this problem as well. My basic view is that if we could decide what laws were passed and how things were done—and we're only one small input into that—I would deregulate a lot of the stuff around the health benefits of AI. I don't worry as much about the chatbot laws. I actually worry more about the drug approval process, where I think AI models are going to greatly accelerate the rate at which we discover drugs, and the pipeline will get jammed up. The pipeline will not be prepared to process all the stuff that's going through it. I think reform of the regulatory process should bias more towards the fact that we have a lot of things coming where the safety and efficacy is actually going to be really crisp and clear, a beautiful thing, and really effective. Maybe we don't need all this superstructure around it that was designed around an era of drugs that barely work and often have serious side effects. At the same time, I think we should be ramping up quite significantly the safety and security legislation. Like I've said, starting with transparency is my view of trying not to hamper the industry, trying to find the right balance. I'm worried about it. Well, basically, I think the last six months and maybe the next few months are going to be about transparency. I think the only way to do this is to be nimble. Now, the legislative process is normally not nimble, but we need to emphasize the urgency of this to everyone involved. That's why I'm sending this message of urgency. That's why I wrote Adolescence of Technology. **Dwarkesh Patel:** Is there anything you can do or advocate that would make it more certain that the benefits of AI are better instantiated? **Dario Amodei:** I don't actually agree that much regarding the developed world. I feel like in the developed world, markets function pretty well. When there's a lot of money to be made on something and it's clearly the best available alternative, it's actually hard for the regulatory system to stop it. We're seeing that in AI itself. A thing I've been trying to fight for is export controls on chips to China. That's in the national security interest of the US. That's squarely within the policy beliefs of almost everyone in Congress of both parties. The case is very clear. The counterarguments against it, I'll politely call them fishy. Yet it doesn't happen and we sell the chips because there's so much money riding on it. That money wants to be made. In that case, in my opinion, that's a bad thing. But it also applies when it's a good thing. So if we're talking about drugs and benefits of the technology, I am not as worried about those benefits being hampered in the developed world. I am a little worried about them going too slow. As I said, I do think we should work to speed the approval process in the FDA. I do think we should fight against these chatbot bills that you're describing. Described individually, I'm against them. I think they're stupid. But I actually think the bigger worry is the developing world, where we don't have functioning markets and where we often can't build on the technology that we've had. I worry more that those folks will get left behind. And I worry that even if the cures are developed, maybe there's someone in rural Mississippi who doesn't get it as well. That's a smaller version of the concern we have in the developing world. So the things we've been doing are working with philanthropists. We work with folks who deliver medicine and health interventions to the developing world, to sub-Saharan Africa, India, Latin America, and other developing parts of the world. That's the thing I think that won't happen on its own.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** You mentioned export controls. Why shouldn't the US and China both have a "country of geniuses in a data center"?

**Dario Amodei:** Why shouldn't it happen. If this does happen, we could have a few situations. If we have an offense-dominant situation, we could have a situation like nuclear weapons, but more dangerous. Either side could easily destroy everything. We could also have a world where it's unstable. The nuclear equilibrium is stable because it's deterrence. But let's say there was uncertainty about, if the two AIs fought, which AI would win? That could create instability. You often have conflict when the two sides have a different assessment of their likelihood of winning. **Dwarkesh Patel:** But this seems like a fully general argument against the diffusion of AI technology.

**Dario Amodei:** That's the implication of this world. Let me just go on, because I think we will get diffusion eventually. The other concern I have is that governments will oppress their own people with AI. I'm worried about a world where you have a country in which there's already a government that's building a high-tech authoritarian state. To be clear, this is about the government. This is not about the people. We need to find a way for people everywhere to benefit. My worry here is about governments. My worry is if the world gets carved up into two pieces, one of those two pieces could be authoritarian or totalitarian in a way that's very difficult to displace. Now, will governments eventually get powerful AI, and is there a risk of authoritarianism? Yes. Will governments eventually get powerful AI, and is there a risk of bad equilibria? Yes, I think both things. But the initial conditions matter. At some point, we're going to need to set up the rules of the road. I'm not saying that one country, either the United States or a coalition of democracies—which I think would be a better setup, although it requires more international cooperation than we currently seem to want to make—should just say, "These are the rules of the road." What I would like is for the democratic nations of the world—those whose governments represent closer to pro-human values—are holding the stronger hand and have more leverage when the rules of the road are set. So I'm very concerned about that initial condition.

**Dwarkesh Patel:** I was re-listening to the interview from three years ago, and one of the ways it aged poorly is that I kept asking questions assuming there was going to be some key fulcrum moment two to three years from now.
